formatted_text:
[COMMENT] BO6 is currently not linux friendly, and I doubt activision cares enough about linux to support it in the future. Is linux 100% gaming ready? I would say yes, but the issue is that triple-a game companies and anticheat devs just dont care enough to support linux, and some games (rainbow 6 for example) will either refuse to boot if the game detects you're on linux or (gtav online for example) will just ban your account if it sees you playing on linux. Linux itself is perfectly fine and capable especially with proton to run windows applications. 

That being said, its a very small number of games that dont work, and if you really want an alternative solution to dual booting you can always create a windows vm and passthrough your gpu and it'll work just fine. 

Overall though, should you switch to linux? I say fuck it, go for it. I went balls deep and made the switch from windows to fedora in 2021, mainly because my pc was "too old" to suppirt windows 11, despite it being a 24 core machine with 128 gigs of ram lol. Proton back then was far more finnicky, and I had to tweak the launch settings for most games to get them to run (and some just didnt at all). Nowadays however, there isnt a single game in my steam library that doesnt just work perfectly fine out of the box on proton 9. Linux gaming has come a long way in the last few years, and its only gonna get better.

formatted_text:
[CONTEXT] Really ? I don't find installing today more accessible than 10 or 15 years ago... For user friendly distros it's basically Boot a live USB, Install, next next next...
Wikis are more or less the same as they were.
Well there maybe more step by step YouTube videos, but they are mostly following the wikis anyway.

The only more accessible thing I find is that gen AI tends to not be that bad at finding how to fix simple issues and is quite good at helping finding the root cause of very specific ones. They tend to BS quite a lot if you trust them too much though. [COMMENT] I think a big thing is that there's *multiple* user friendly distros now, where 10-16 years ago distros like Linux Mint stood out for doing such innovative things like bundling Nvidia drivers so users can actually use their computer without knowing what the fuck a GPU is.  A lot more stuff has a GUI, immutables along with Flatpaks are *really* reslient against user error (Steam Decks in particular are surviving fine in the hands of users who have no idea what Linux is, even if they go into desktop mode), Wayland's progressed to where a lot more types of displays and configurations are handled nicely out of the box, Pipewire has resolved many audio issues, GPU support has improved *dramatically* with Nvidia in particular now sorta playing ball, the major DE's have had major improvements.

Sure, it's hard to argue that anythign could be quite as important as a distro installing via a GUI installer, if a distro does not have that then it's absurd to call it accessible (*maybe* you could make an exception for a TUI installer, but not having mouse support is gonna confuse some number of people).  But while that's a very *visible* improvement Linux distros made way back in the day when Ubuntu first came out with a GUI installer, there's been a ton of stuff happening in the background that has removed a lot of the pain points since then.

formatted_text:
[COMMENT] Okay question as an ECE worker/teacher because this entire thread is starting to confuse me: is it a double standard and only "rules for thee but not for me" when the singular person different from the pack is benefiting?

Because to me this *is* an accommodation for OP's child. People lost sight of what the word means. It's not instant gratification or making every little inconvenience go away forever. It's not "you have to let my kid do whatever they want and give them everything INSTANTLY because they're autistic there can be NO HICCUPS!!!!"

If the norm is everyone can bring a teddy, but this little girl is on the spectrum and it causes problems when she brings hers (and she's the only one like this), why again would the entire class have to stop? That sounds like trying to center the world around your kid just because some things are inevitably uncomfortable or difficult to understand at the ripe age of 3 (which is true for everyone, not just ND kids.) That just sounds like punishing the mass for the status of one. Which is just as bad as not accommodating ND children properly imo. It can cause resentment from the other kids, which OP's daughter does not deserve, and doesn't give OP's daughter **a proper way to learn emotional regulation and that sometimes things can't go our way in life. And if anyone is blatantly arguing against that, please seek help and maybe do some research as well. And also don't have (more) kids.** The world is already full of self-centered, inconsiderate assholes and we don't need aaaany more.

Saw someone on here saying "I hate when parents try to make their kids be grateful" like there's no extra context, no more to that comment, nothing. And they were being deadass. Hate the current trendy selfishness-is-the-BEST!!! mindsets floating around.

If everyone was allowed to bring rubber bands to school for silly fridays, and my daughter was the only one flicking people with them, of course it makes sense to remove her, one child, from being able to participate until they improve or can show signs of readiness for this particular situation (which would be the teddy bear ofc).

Let's not pretend like it's not okay to make exceptions and handle things on a case-by-case situation. Getting the same treatment classwide is inappropriate if all the kids aren't on the same playing field. If your kid is the only freaking the fuck out over a bear, they don't get it. Which is not a punishment btw. But for everyone else it would be, so why would we do that to them even though they can mentally handle bringing one and behave with it? Simple as.

formatted_text:
[COMMENT] The criticism about the drivers, at the very least for the video card or IGP is valid. It is so needless complicated compared to how Windows handles it, which is barely by giving a generic driver so you get video output on your display and then let's your GPU manufacturer provide the driver. They don't have driver support for that card for that version of Windows? Well there you go, it's the card manufacturer's fault. They do have a driver? Just download and install, ez peasy lemon squeezy.

  
On Linux?

  
If you have nvidia card and the particular distro either offers a version of the install with nvidia proprietary drivers during installation or you'lll have to figure out a post install way of adding them with terminal commands. Who tf expects a casual PC user to use terminal commands for basic things like installing video card drivers?

  
Then there is the trouble shooting issue. Suppose you have AMD card and it's supposed to just work but it doesn't. Maybe, like trouble shooting for nvidia, the card is too new of a realease for the distro/lernel version you installed or the card is too old and the driver support was discontinued AND the Linux distro version has no easy way or any way to install older drivers. For new cards, the solution is easy, install a rolling release distro with new kernel and drivers. What about the guys with older cards? Well fuck them, that's Linux's answer for casual users because there is no obvious way what they should do and the tutorials they do find are likely out of date or worse they work but there are lots of bugs and because there is no official support for them, none of those bugs will get solved. Worst still the issues will keep increasing as they install more programs and try to change GUI settings that work for others fine but crashes their system or gets them a blank screen instead.

  
I have been using Windows for 20 years and Linux on and off again for over 10 years and even I don't really understand the Linux video card driver, what is supported and what is not supported, how to troubleshoot, how to handle IGPs or mobile GPUs or solve issues like for example having both an AMD IGP and dedicated GPU and games default to the integrated one. Even worse, imagine now that Intel makes dedicated GPUs and you have one of those fucking things AND and Intel IGP and the software you run defaults to the IGP. What do you do?



 I don't get it and I say that not being ashamed but due to Linux's approach to drivers being shit and subpar to Windows way of doing things. By fuck also stop crying about Wayland replacing X11, that should have happened in 2005 not 2025.

formatted_text:
[CONTEXT] I think a big thing is that there's *multiple* user friendly distros now, where 10-16 years ago distros like Linux Mint stood out for doing such innovative things like bundling Nvidia drivers so users can actually use their computer without knowing what the fuck a GPU is.  A lot more stuff has a GUI, immutables along with Flatpaks are *really* reslient against user error (Steam Decks in particular are surviving fine in the hands of users who have no idea what Linux is, even if they go into desktop mode), Wayland's progressed to where a lot more types of displays and configurations are handled nicely out of the box, Pipewire has resolved many audio issues, GPU support has improved *dramatically* with Nvidia in particular now sorta playing ball, the major DE's have had major improvements.

Sure, it's hard to argue that anythign could be quite as important as a distro installing via a GUI installer, if a distro does not have that then it's absurd to call it accessible (*maybe* you could make an exception for a TUI installer, but not having mouse support is gonna confuse some number of people).  But while that's a very *visible* improvement Linux distros made way back in the day when Ubuntu first came out with a GUI installer, there's been a ton of stuff happening in the background that has removed a lot of the pain points since then. [COMMENT] > where 10-16 years ago distros like Linux Mint stood out for doing such innovative things like bundling Nvidia drivers so users can actually use their computer without knowing what the fuck a GPU is.

that made me laugh, but it's exactly why I first actually started seriously using mint as a desktop back then... I had tried a couple distros over the years (too many years, I'm old), and usually got frustrated and gave up

[CONTEXT] I hear these job security seeking devops fools trying to justify this by saying, "It would take 1000 developers 1 billion hours to save even $1 of AWS costs, so it just isn't worth it."

Not only is this often wrong, but there can be other benefits; such as a great piece of highly efficient low running cost code can be copied. This can be used in maybe a dozen other features which, otherwise, weren't worth the ongoing running costs.

Also, if you keep things tight and fast, whole features which just weren't going to be responsive enough in real time, can potentially be created.

Also, opex is what often kills a company; not capex. Knowing which is best spent where  and when is not the job of Devops fools. [COMMENT] Fuck all the mini therapy sessions, he’s 3 and doesn’t understand 80% of what you say. You’re the adult, you control yourself. Your patience HAS TO outlast their will. There’s no other approach.

You model the behavior you want, he hits and yells? Hold his hands down softly “no hitting” in a soft voice. And just move on. This idea that you can have a full blown lecture with a 3-year old is absolutely ridiculous. They are growing, everything is new to them. Maybe give them a hug to let them know it’s all good.

The other point to touch on is being embarrassed when you go out. He’s 3, if anyone gets annoyed, they need to leave. I don’t suggest taking kids who can’t control themselves to a fancy outing but if your local chic-fil-a has a play place, let the them loose after they’re done eating. But only after they’re done eating. He’s a kid, don’t ever be embarrassed of your own kid, let other people worry about themselves and why a 3-year olds actions bug them. Maybe they should have been so lucky to have a parent like you who tries and keeps going even when it seems like what they are doing is useless. 

For the record I have the same stats, times 2. I’ll tell you this much, you’re doing the right things, just need to tweak your perspective. You’re there to help your kids and raise them to be adults, they need you to show them the way. Other people’s opinions and judgements are non of your business, you’re there for your kids. Keep going, it gets better.
